questionText: Starting from the addition formulae $\sin(A+B)=\sin A\cos B+\cos A\sin B$ and $\cos(A+B)=\cos A\cos B-\sin A\sin B$, show by setting $B=A$ that $\sin 2A = 2\sin A\cos A$ and $\cos 2A = \cos^2 A - \sin^2 A$.

steps.explanation: We begin with the addition formula for sine, which is our known starting result.
Because $2A = A+A$, a double angle is just a sum of two equal angles, so we replace every $B$ with $A$.

steps.description: Simplify to the sine double angle
steps.explanation: The two terms on the right are identical, so they add to give twice $\sin A\cos A$.
Now take the addition formula for cosine as the starting point for the second identity.
Again set $B=A$ so that the sum $A+A$ becomes the double angle $2A$.

steps.description: Simplify to the cosine double angle
steps.explanation: Writing $\cos A\cos A=\cos^2 A$ and $\sin A\sin A=\sin^2 A$ gives the standard form of $\cos 2A$.
Using $\sin^2 A+\cos^2 A=1$ to replace one of the squares gives two further useful versions of the same identity.
These are exactly the double-angle formulae, now shown to be the special case $B=A$ of the addition formulae, as required.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: By setting $B=A$ we obtain $\sin 2A = 2\sin A\cos A$ and $\cos 2A = \cos^2 A - \sin^2 A$ (equivalently $2\cos^2 A-1$ or $1-2\sin^2 A$).
